title: The risk and return of impact investing funds
abstract: We provide the first analysis of the risk exposure and risk-adjusted performance of impact investing funds, private market funds with dual financial and social goals. We introduce a dataset of impact fund cash flows and exploit distortions in VC performance measures to characterize risk profiles. Impact funds have a lower market β than comparable private market strategies. Accounting for β, impact funds underperform the public market, though not necessarily more so than comparable strategies. We consider alternative pricing models, accounting for sustainability and emerging markets risk. We show investors’ wealth portfolios and taste change the perceived financial merit of impact investing.

article.1. Introduction: Impact investing has attracted a growing amount of investor capital over the last decade by promising to target both financial returns and a social or environmental goal. It has simultaneously attracted substantial debate, especially over its financial merit. Critics argue that constrained strategies like impact investing must have lower risk-adjusted returns than unconstrained strategies under standard assumptions (e.g. perfect and complete capital markets, rational and informed investors) (Brest et al., 2018, Barber et al., 2021). Others challenge the validity of these assumptions, especially in private markets (Cole et al., 2021).1 Crucially missing from the debate in private markets is an understanding of impact investing’s risk exposure, which has the potential to reconcile concessionary absolute returns with a profitable strategy for some investors, depending on the risks they wish to hedge.This paper fills the gap in the literature by providing the first analysis of the risk properties of impact investing relative to other strategies and the corresponding risk-adjusted return. Using new data and methodology, we find impact investing has lower exposure to market risk relative to other private market strategies, providing support to the hedging view of ESG. After accounting for the high market risk exposure of most private market funds, returns are closer between impact investing and non-impact investing. Still, if investors care only about total returns, impact funds will represent a financial return concession. The reference point matters too: the financial return concession can be larger for investors who hold different wealth portfolios than the market. Not only do our findings demonstrate when a constrained strategy like impact investing can extract value, but they also illuminate the role that impact can play in modifying the risk profile of investors’ portfolios.Studying the risk and return of impact investing presents several empirical challenges. First, there is no publicly available dataset ofimpact fund cash flows. Second, private market funds (e.g., venture capital (VC), buyout, etc.) tend to have infrequent and highly skewed payoffs, making traditional linear factor modeling techniques tricky. Small sample sizes and young funds in the impact investing space exacerbate these problems.We address the data challenge by creating a new dataset of impact investing cash flows, combining data from the Impact Finance Research Consortium (IFRC) and Preqin.2 First, as part of the IFRC effort, we collect annual and quarterly financial statements directly from impact funds and convert them into a standardized database covering contributions and distributions net of fees, as well as net asset value (NAV), among other variables. This dataset will be made available to researchers through application to the IFRC.3 Second, we leverage the comprehensive list of past and existing impact funds created by the IFRC, and search for these funds in Preqin. Our final sample consists of 94 funds covering vintage years 1999 through 2015, with cash flow data from 1999 to 2021.We address the methodological challenges by proposing a new statistic to infer beta from only cash flows. Our methodology does not suffer from the common critiques of NAV-based return measures (Korteweg, 2019) and relies on few assumptions. We leverage the Generalized Public Market Equivalent of Korteweg and Nagel (2016), an extension of the Public Market Equivalent (PME) first proposed by Kaplan and Schoar (2005). As Korteweg and Nagel (2016) show, although the PME has many useful features for assessing the performance of VC, it systematically overestimates the performance of high-beta assets in times of rising public equity markets relative to a one-factor CAPM model. Our key insight is to use the fact that this bias grows with the asset’s market β: the distance between PME and GPME (the “PME wedge”) thus informs us about the market β of the underlying asset. If impact investing has a positive PME wedge, it implies that the market β of impact investing is greater than one, and the greater the wedge, the greater the implied β. We further extend the approach to two other factors relevant to impact investing, a public sustainability factor and an emerging markets factor.A remaining challenge is that any PME wedge we estimate for impact investing may be driven by general private market trends, asset class, or timing, in addition to the impact component. To solve this problem, we construct benchmark fund groups using non-impact fund cash flows from Preqin in our main analysis, and non-impact fund cash flows from Burgiss in the Online Appendix. We first match each impact fund in our sample to a benchmark fund with the same vintage, asset type, and closest size. Perfectly controlling for everything outside impact is not possible, but this set of matched funds helps us to address the effect of characteristics known to influence risk exposure. To provide context from a well-understood asset class, we also benchmark to US-based VC funds, because VC is the asset class most representative of impact funds overall4 and its risk and return properties have been studied extensively (Korteweg, 2019). A limitation of this second benchmark is that the comparison to VC funds is not apples-to-apples and may be influenced by asset class, vintage, and size.We document three main findings about the risk and return properties of impact investing. First, impact investing is less sensitive to movements in public equity markets than matched funds, and much less sensitive when compared to VC funds. Our results still imply a market β greater than one, as the PME overestimates impact performance relative to the GPME in our time period. When we artificially lever up our impact fund cash flows, the PME wedge increases, also consistent with a positive β that grows as the strategy is levered up. However, this wedge grows more slowly for impact funds than for matched or VC funds. A portfolio that goes long $1 in matched or VC funds and short $1 in impact still has a positive β, providing further evidence of greater market risk exposure for matched and VC funds than for impact. Our results indicate that shifting comparable private market investments to impact would reduce an investor’s overall market risk exposure.Second, we find impact funds have lower total returns than benchmark non-impact funds, but the gap in financial performance is smaller after accounting for market risk exposure. Our estimates imply that impact funds underperform the public market on a risk-adjusted basis by −$0.47 per $1 invested, compared to −$0.33 per $1 invested for matched funds. A value-weighted portfolio long in matched funds and short impact yields a market risk-adjusted return of −$0.015, not statistically different from zero. The negative returns of both strategies reflect in part the general underperformance of VC-type funds relative to public markets in recent decades (Harris et al., 2014, Gupta and Van Nieuwerburgh, 2021). Our VC benchmark underperforms the public market by an average $0.43 per $1 invested over the same time period, again after removing the component of performance explained by β, and a value-weighted portfolio long $1 in VC and short $1 in impact funds has a negative market risk-adjusted return of −$0.16 during our sample. Our results imply that if investors care about market risk-adjusted returns, impact funds are not necessarily concessionary relative to other private market funds.Third, we characterize how investor taste and alternative wealth portfolios affect our assessment of impact investing’s role in investor portfolios. We start by building on Pastor et al. (2021) and explore the extent to which ESG “taste” in the market can help explain the returns to impact investing. Adding a public sustainability factor to our model improves our ability to explain returns for impact funds, but not for matched or VC funds. Next, we consider alternatives to the public market as the best representation of investors’ wealth portfolios. For investors whose return on wealth is best represented by the return on a public sustainability index or by the return on an emerging markets index, impact funds yield a larger financial return concession relative to non-impact benchmarks. Both impact and matched funds appear to have relatively low risk exposure to the public sustainability index. However, impact funds are much more exposed to the emerging markets factor than matched funds. Our findings paint a more comprehensive picture of impact investing risk and performance for investors with a potentially different wealth portfolio than the market portfolio, and suggest that the financial merit of impact investing will be different for different types of investors.Our work contributes to the broader understanding of socially responsible investing (SRI) and environmental, social, and governance (ESG) factors. Most existing work has focused on public markets, exploring investor taste for “green” strategies (Hartzmark and Sussman, 2019, Krueger et al., 2020, Pastor et al., 2021, Fama and French, 2007) and the pricing of ESG factors (Ilhan et al., 2021, Bansal et al., 2022). Renneboog et al. (2008) provide an overview of SRI in mutual funds. We extend this work to private markets, which present a different set of challenges and opportunities for sustainability-minded investors. Companies seeking capital in the impact investing market are typically orders of magnitude smaller than public companies, with completely different expected growth paths and risk considerations. The potential for impact is also distinct from public market investments. Investors, via funds, have substantially more influence over the development of portfolio companies than they would in public companies.In the public market literature, the covariance of SRI and ESG strategies with the market is ambiguous. Bansal et al. (2022) find that SRI is highly pro-cyclical; however, Nofsinger and Varma (2014) and Pastor and Vorsatz (2020) find evidence that sustainable mutual funds outperform during market crises. Dunn et al. (2018) suggest that stocks with higher ESG exposure have lower risk. We show that impact investing in private markets is pro-cyclical, but less so than comparable non-impact strategies. We document impact investing’s distinct risk characteristics along other dimensions as well, such as relative to a public sustainability factor and an emerging markets factor.Data limitations have kept the literature on impact investing sparse, but this paper joins a few others in the burgeoning literature on impact investing in private markets. On the descriptive side, Burton et al. (2021) introduce a new database on investor and firm characteristics in impact investing, and provide essential statistics to understand this emerging space; Geczy et al. (2021) detail the contracting and governance practices of impact investing funds.5 On the theory side, Chowdhry et al., 2019, Oehmke and Opp, 2022, Green and Roth, 2021, and Gupta et al. (2022) propose models for the existence and usefulness of impact investing. Two other papers study the financial performance of private impact investing funds, without explicitly addressing risk exposure: Barber et al. (2021) use a willingness-to-pay model to show that investors accept 2.5%–3.7% lower internal rates of return (IRRs) for impact funds, and Cole et al. (2021) find that a large impact investor’s long-run returns outperformed the market by 15% over nearly seven decades of investing activity. We provide a new perspective to these two papers by addressing the risk exposure behind these performance measures, decomposing the component of impact returns that can be explained by systematic risk, a public sustainability factor, and an emerging market index. In our sample, impact underperforms relative to public markets, but not always more so than comparable strategies after accounting for market risk exposure. Another advantage of our data is that we differentiate impact funds that are explicitly concessionary from funds that state their intention to achieve market-rate returns. Our results pertain to the latter fund type only.Last, this paper contributes to a rich asset pricing literature on the risk and return of PE and VC cash flows, starting with Cochrane (2005) and Korteweg and Sorensen (2010). More recently, Ang et al. (2018) estimate a PE-specific return series using a Bayesian approach and assuming a linear factor structure. Gupta and Van Nieuwerburgh (2021) propose a new method to risk-adjust PE returns and estimate factor risk exposures for each cash flow horizon. We build heavily on the GPME approach introduced by Korteweg and Nagel (2016), leveraging the distortions that they document to back out the risk properties of impact and VC funds. This approach allows us to directly compare VC and impact funds in an intuitive way. A key difference between this method and Gupta and Van Nieuwerburgh (2021) is that the latter uses expectations of stochastic discount factors (SDFs) from dividend strip prices, while we rely on a realized SDF.
article.2. Data: One of the challenges in studying impact investing is the lack of data. We overcome this challenge by creating a new dataset of impact investing cash flows from new and preexisting data sources, which we describe below. We then describe the non-impact funds we use to benchmark our results, and provide summary statistics for both impact and benchmark funds. We also describe the data for the public market replicating portfolios used in the GPME approach. More details regarding the sample and construction are in Appendix A.2.1. Impact investing fundsWe start with a list of impact investing funds compiled by members of the IFRC, a collaboration across the Wharton School of Business, Harvard Business School, and the University of Chicago’s Booth School of Business. The list of funds comes from multiple databases, cross-checked with industry experts, published reports, and other independent research (Burton et al., 2021). From this list, we obtain financial data in two ways: directly from funds via the IFRC’s data collection (“IFD funds”), and indirectly from funds on the list, which we match to cash flow data from Preqin (“Preqin impact funds”). We describe each source in detail in Online Appendix I.B.1. Here we provide descriptive statistics for the combined sample, followed by a discussion of potential selection issues and differences between the two groups that make up our sample.Impact funds include funds that target market-rate returns, as well as funds that target a positive but below-market return. For this paper, we focus exclusively on market rate-seeking funds, so as not to bias the results towards finding below market returns (Cole et al., 2021).6 We also limit our analysis to funds operating for at least three years, and with no data gaps at the annual level. Finally, we conduct an independent check to verify that all funds in this sample state a clear impact goal, either on their websites or through legal documents they have shared with the IFRC. Our final analysis sample consists of 94 funds, of which 48 are IFD funds and 46 Preqin impact funds.7Our fund sample covers vintage years 1999 through 2015, with cash flow data through the end of 2021. We use quarterly data if available (87 funds), otherwise annual data (7 funds) (using annual rather than quarterly cash flows does not substantially alter results). Cash flows reflect returns to LPs net of fees, except for the final distribution when funds are not fully liquidated at the end of our sample. Because impact fund term lengths are typically ten years, like other private market funds, we follow Harris et al. (2014) and Korteweg and Nagel (2016) and use the last period NAV as the final distribution for funds that are not fully liquidated, with the caveat that this final cash flow does not account for future fees. We address the concerns of using last-period NAV as final distribution in Section 2.3.Funds in our sample target both social and environmental objectives, often in line with the United Nations’ Sustainable Development Goals (Table 1).8 Funds often have multiple goals, as evidenced in Panel A by the fact that environmental objectives are frequently paired with social objectives, but we assign each fund to a primary focus for simplicity. We present the summary of these stated impact goals in Panel B of Table 1. The modal mission focus is economic development, where funds aim to “mobilize capital to help improve the socioeconomic condition of underserved people”. Another common focus is sustainable practices, where funds incorporate both environmental and social considerations in their sourcing and governance of portfolio companies. Clean technology and energy is the third most common focus.The focus on economic development is comparable to the most commonly stated impact goals of the funds studied in Barber et al. (2021), particularly small and medium-sized enterprise (SME) funding and regional development. Similarly, they also find about a quarter of their impact funds include a focus on environmental impact. Among our funds, environmentally-focused funds are about 20% of the sample.Table 1. Mission focus for sample impact funds.We collect information on mission focus from a combination of sustainability reports, prospectuses, and websites. In Panel A, we provide the breakdown across social, environmental, and combination social/environmental mission focus. In Panel B, we provide further detail on the primary mission focus of the fund. Funds often have multiple goals; for example, funds whose primary mission is economic development often have sustainable practices or diversity, equity and inclusion (DEI) as a secondary focus. For this table, we assign each fund to a single primary focus. Primary mission focus labels are defined as follows: economic development refers to a primary objective of improving economic conditions and employment in underserved regions; sustainable practices incorporate both environmental and social considerations in the sourcing and governance of portfolio companies; clean technology and energy funds invest primarily in clean technology and renewable energy companies; social services funds invest primarily in sectors that provide social goods to underserved populations, such as healthcare, education, or real estate; other environment includes sustainable infrastructure, water, and more general green innovation; DEI focuses on supporting women and underrepresented minorities; and food and agriculture focuses on food security and sustainable agriculture.Panel A: Mission categoryNum. fundsEnvironmental18Social48Social and environmental28Total94Panel B: Primary mission focusNum. fundsClean technology and renewable energy14Diversity, equity, and inclusion4Economic development31Financial inclusion and microfinance9Food and agriculture2Other environment5Social services8Sustainable practices21Total94Table 2. One concern with IFD funds is that they could be positively selected, with successful funds more likely to share their documents with the IFRC. IRRs are higher for IFD than Preqin impact funds, but multiples and PME are similar for both samples. We also note that when compared to Burgiss, an alternative source of private market data, Preqin VC funds appear to be somewhat negatively selected. We anticipate that the combination of Preqin and IFD impact funds balances out potential selection biases in either direction, though of course we cannot demonstrate this without observing the full universe of data. Since our main results focus on risk profiles, our most important challenge is potential selection bias on β. Given the difficulty in calculating β and the fact that this is not something funds report to investors or regularly measure for themselves, it seems unlikely that funds self-select on this measure.2.2. Non-impact fundsWe contrast impact investing funds with a set of private market funds matched to impact funds on asset class, vintage, and size. This benchmarking aims to isolate the influence of the impact component on the risk–return relationship as much as possible, by controlling for salient characteristics that could affect the risk–return relationship. Our thought experiment is: if an investor has a choice between investing $1 in an impact fund and $1 in an otherwise comparable non-impact fund, what are the implications of this choice for risk and return?In addition to the matched funds, we also report results for non-impact VC funds in the US (including generalist, early stage, and late stage). The purpose of this second benchmark is to contextualize results relative to a well-documented asset class that is also the most commonly occurring fund type in the broader universe of impact funds. Not only does VC represent 43% of impact funds raised since 2007 (Wiek et al., 2022), but VC is also the most commonly occurring asset class in our sample of impact funds (at around 40% of funds, discussed in the next section). VC funds also share salient characteristics with impact funds, such as a focus on equity investments and minority stakes. These features are why VC has been used as a benchmark for impact funds in other work instead of asset classes like buyout (Cole et al., 2022, Geczy et al., 2021).Brown et al. (2015) document strengths and limitations of different commercially available data sets for private market cash flows. We present our main results using benchmark cash flows from Preqin. A strength of Preqin is its accessibility, but a potential weakness is that its sample can be prone to survivorship bias and selection issues (Preqin VC funds tend to display worse performance than Burgiss funds, for example (Brown et al., 2015)). Since we draw on Preqin for part of our impact sample, we use Preqin as our main benchmark, but we show our results are robust to using benchmark cash flows from Burgiss in Online Appendix I.B.2.1. Burgiss provides information management services to institutional investors, and makes anonymized data from these funds accessible to researchers. Burgiss data therefore tends to be less sensitive to survivorship bias and has broader representation. We find matched and VC funds in Burgiss tend to perform slightly better than their counterparts in Preqin. We cannot use Burgiss to augment our sample of impact funds because Burgiss anonymizes funds, and we cannot identify impact funds in their sample.9We construct our matched fund benchmark by matching each impact fund to a different fund in Preqin based on general asset class, vintage, and closest size. Because Preqin asset classes are slightly different than the asset classes in the impact sample, we make the following adjustments: we match generalist equity impact funds to balanced Preqin funds and impact generalist, debt, and real estate funds to Preqin general venture funds. We allow for funds with a non-US fund focus, but find that most of the matched sample has a focus on the US. We cut off cash flows for each matched fund based on the final cash flow date for the matching impact fund. We convert the cash flows to quarterly frequency to better match the structure of the impact fund data.To construct the general VC benchmark, we follow Korteweg and Nagel (2016) and restrict the sample of Preqin funds to funds with at least $5 million in assets and with a US focus. We also remove any impact funds that we can identify. To mirror the time period of our impact fund sample, we include VC funds with vintages from 1999 through 2015 and cut off cash flows after 2021. We also drop funds with less than three years of data.2.3. Descriptive statistics for impact and benchmark fundsImpact funds invest across a diverse array of geographies, industries, and even asset classes, more so than other comparable private market funds. Fig. 1 compares the distribution of portfolio company headquarter regions between impact, matched, and VC funds. The prevalence of impact investments in emerging market economies is a distinguishing feature of impact, and likely contributes to the difference in risk profile from matched and VC funds. This fact motivates our analysis of the EME risk factor in Section 5. Fig. 2 reports on the industry focus of the impact funds in our sample.10 Both figures demonstrate substantial variety across our sample, especially when compared to matched and VC funds.Additionally, impact funds invest across private market asset classes. We plot the frequencies of asset classes for the impact fund sample in Fig. 3. The most common asset class in our sample of impact funds is VC, followed by expansion capital, buyout, and generalist equity. Mezzanine, real assets, and other funds are also represented in the sample, but in much smaller numbers. Lastly, Fig. 4 shows the sequence number of the funds in our sample. We address fund liquidation status by matching on vintage: both impact and matched funds that are not fully liquidated have NAV as their final period distribution payout. We show that while NAV does tend to overestimate future cash flows, this overestimation is similar across impact, matched, and VC samples. Based on this analysis, we conclude that the use of NAV as a final distribution for young funds will not affect the conclusions we make about the relative risk and performance between the three samples. We further check that our main results are not driven by young funds’ final NAV distribution, by limiting analysis to funds with at least seven years of data.2.4. Public market replicating portfoliosTo compute the GPME, we require public market portfolios that replicate the timing of capital accumulations and payouts of our private market funds. We use the one-month T-bill for the risk free rate and the CRSP value-weighted index for the market return. For additional factors, we use the Dow-Jones Sustainability World Index (DJSI World) and the MSCI Emerging Markets IMI index. The DJSI World represents the top 10% of companies in the S&P Global BMI based on their S&P Global ESG scores. The index is also one of the oldest sustainability benchmarks, beginning in 1999 at the start of our sample. The MSCI Emerging Markets Investable Market Index includes small, large, and mid-cap companies from 24 emerging market economies. The T-bill rate and CRSP value-weighted index are from Ken French’s website. The DJSI World is extracted from the Bloomberg terminal. The MSCI Emerging Markets IMI index is from FactSet.
article.3. Characterizing risk and return in pri: A key issue in measuring risk in private equity is that cash flows are irregularly spaced, making regression factor analysis difficult. We leverage two different models of stochastic discount factors implied by existing measures of performance in the private equity literature. Our insight is to use the gap between these performance measures to create a statistic that captures risk exposure from cash flows. We call this statistic the PME wedge.3.1. Public Market Equivalent (PME)We start with the discount rate implied by the PME, which is equivalently the SDF for an investor with log-utility preferences (Kaplan and Schoar, 2005, Sorensen and Jagannathan, 2015). Online Appendix I.A.1 provides a detailed review of the PME for readers less familiar with the measure. The PME discount rate from time t to t+1 can be represented as a stochastic discount factor of the following form: (1)Mt+1=exp(−logRt+1m)where Rt+1m is the gross return on the market portfolio. When the cash flows of a fund are discounted using this SDF, the PME has the interpretation of an excess return relative to the market. The market return is a proxy for the return on the investor’s overall wealth portfolio.While the PME has many useful features, it also has limitations, which we leverage for our purposes. Kaplan and Schoar (2005) originally note that the PME implicitly assumes that systematic risk β is equal to one.11 Korteweg and Nagel (2016) show that under the assumption of jointly log-normal returns, the SDF of the PME implies the following risk–return relationship: (2)logE[Rt+1]=logE[Rm,t+1]−σm2+βσm2This SDF restricts the equity premium to the variance of log market return σm2 and the (log) risk-free rate to logE[Rm,t+1]−σm2. Korteweg and Nagel (2016)’s observation is that this relationship is inconsequential for assets with β=1, for which the risk–return relationship reduces to logE[Rt+1]=logE[Rm,t+1]. However, for assets with β different from one, PME can be distortionary. Korteweg and Nagel (2016) argue this is particularly a concern during times of strongly rising equity markets, when logE[Rm,t+1]−logRf>σm2. The SDF will then not accurately adjust for market risk exposure, leading PME estimates to be excessively high for high β assets when equity markets are rising.3.2. Generalized Public Market Equivalent (GPME)We create a new statistic by comparing the PME with the Generalized PME (GPME) from Korteweg and Nagel (2016). The GPME is conceptually similar to the PME, but the single-period SDF used to discount PE fund cash flows takes the more flexible form: (3)Mt+1∗=exp(a−brm,t+1)where rm,t+1 is the log market return at t+1.Under this flexible SDF, the PME is a special case when a=0 and b=1. When a and b are estimated to reflect the market return and risk-free rate, assuming jointly log-normal returns, this SDF implies the following log-linear β pricing relationship that appropriately accounts for market risk exposure. (4)logE[Rt+1]=rf+β(logE[Rm,t+1]−rf)We provide more details on GPME and describe the estimation procedure for parameters a and b in Section 4.1 and Appendix A.The GPME takes a benchmarking perspective, asking how an investment adds value to an investor’s portfolio that would not be attainable from the market. Instead of using a ratio as in the classic PME formulation in Kaplan and Schoar (2005), the GPME in Korteweg and Nagel (2016) is defined as the sum of discounted distributions minus the sum of discounted contributions, using the multi-period discount rate: (5)GPMEi=∑j=1JMt+h(j)∗,h(j)Distributionsi,t+h(j)−∑j=1JMt+h(j)∗,h(j)Callsi,t+h(j)Each cash flow is discounted to time t, including the initial investment. Moreover, each cash flow is normalized by the size of the fund. Because of this, the GPME can be described as the NPV of $1 invested in the fund. Under the null hypothesis of no abnormal performance, E[GPMEi]=0.The GPME has its own limitations. Gredil et al. (2020) show that an NPV-based estimator of fund performance may suffer more bias for samples with longer fund life, something we confirm in Online Appendix I.C.3. Nonetheless, we show in Online Appendix I.B.2.4 that our results are not due to different fund lives for our benchmark funds. We also show in Online Appendix I.C.1 and I.C.2 that our approach is robust to concerns about finite sample performance and number of cash flows.Another limitation is the noisiness of individual fund GPME estimates. To solve this statistical issue, Korteweg and Nagel (2022) propose a new performance measure, α, which is less sensitive to noise in individual fund cash flows while still preserving the aggregate pricing implications of GPME. However, this approach imposes stronger assumptions which make it ill-suited to comparing risk exposure across strategies. We discuss this approach in Appendix B and show that it results in β estimates that are consistent with our wedge approach. We also use this approach to plot the dispersion of individual asset performance for different asset classes in Section 4.1.3.3. The PME wedge intuitionWe use the difference between the PME and GPME (the “PME wedge”) to inform us about the β of an underlying asset, where β is the covariance with a factor representing an investor’s wealth portfolio, such as the public market. For this purpose, the GPME is defined as in Eq. (5), and the PME is defined as the special case of the GPME where the SDF parameters in Eq. (3) are restricted to a=0 and b=1. (6)E[PME wedgei]≡E[PMEi]−E[GPMEi]One of the advantages of this approach is that it requires relatively few assumptions. The PME wedge does not rely on either of the models being the true model of expected returns. The only assumption needed for our statistic to adequately capture β is that private equity returns and factor returns are jointly log-normal.Assuming that the public market and the private fund gross returns are jointly log-normal, the PME SDF in expression (1) implies a pricing model with a log expected return as in expression (2). Our sample measure of E[PMEi] is then the pricing error of this log-utility model. Similarly, the GPME SDF formulation (3) implies a pricing model with log expected return in expression (4). Our sample measure E[GPMEi] is the pricing error for a log-linear CAPM model.In the case of the PME, a non-zero pricing error reflects two components: one is a true α, which may not be captured by the expected return of a CAPM model, and the other is the expected return difference between the PME (as in expression (2)) and GPME (as in expression (4)). The PME wedge, which takes the difference between E[PMEi] and E[GPMEi], removes any true α component not captured by either model, and only implies an expected return difference between the PME and GPME.Both models can have mispricing in terms of pricing error, but by taking the difference between the two pricing errors, our wedge estimate is one-to-one with the following expression under log-normality, where β=Cov(rm,t+1,ri,t+1)Var(rm,t+1): (7)(β−1)(logE[Rm,t+1]−rf−σm2)Since we do not need either SDF to be the true SDF, our approach creates a model-free statistic. The exponential affine SDF format is useful for our purposes because it directly relates the PME wedge to β, but it is not a modeling assumption. In particular, whenever we measure this wedge over a period when (logE[Rm,t+1]−rf−σm2)≠0, the wedge is roughly proportional to the deviation of β from a value of 1. Because a and b are chosen to exactly price the market and risk-free asset, the wedge recovers the β of private market fund cash flows with the market return.The ideal way to assess risk exposure and market β would be to run factor regressions and directly measure covariances. However, in the face of data challenges from private market cash flows, we prefer to use our PME wedge to infer the risk exposure for the following reasons. First, the PME wedge is easy to compute in the sense that it compares the net present value of cash flows with two different discount rates. Furthermore, assessing risk using only one of these net present values is impossible; we need two measures that behave in reliably consistent ways (see Section 3.4). Second, this approach backs out risk exposure using only fund cash flows data, avoiding the many issues caused by irregularly spaced cash flows or using NAVs to form a consistent return series.12 Third, the measure only relies on the assumption that public market index and private equity log returns are jointly normal, which is a standard assumption in SDF-based asset pricing.3.4. Prediction developmentWe now explain how we can characterize risk and performance properties of different asset classes using the properties of the PME wedge. We start with predictions regarding market risk, then move to covariance with other factors. Again, GPME is defined as in Eq. (5), and the PME is the special case of the GPME where the SDF parameters in Eq. (3) are restricted to a=0 and b=1.3.4.1. Market risk predictionsIn periods of rising equity markets (i.e., states of the world when the equity premium is higher than what is assumed under the PME), the PME systematically overestimates the performance of high-β assets when compared to the GPME, and underestimates the performance of low-β assets.Assuming jointly log-normal private-equity and market returns, the difference between PME and GPME, the “PME wedge”, directly reflects a sample estimate of the estimator in expression (7). This flows directly from rearranging Eqs. (2), (4).13 Notice that this difference reduces to zero when β is one. When β is different than one, our predictions depend on the magnitude of the equity premium.We say the market equity premium is sufficiently high if (8)(logE[Rm,t+1]−rf−σm2)>0where logE[Rm,t+1] is the log expected gross return of the market portfolio and σm2 is the variance of the log gross returns of the market portfolio. While this is derived under the assumption of jointly log-normal returns, it should be approximately true in the data. This definition characterizes how much the equity premium observed ex post deviates from what is assumed by the log-utility PME model. Our first prediction formalizes the behavior of the SDF when the equity premium is sufficiently high.Prediction 1 P1SDF. When the equity premium is sufficiently high, the parameters of the SDF will not be those of the log-utility SDF. That is, for an SDF of the form Mt+1∗=exp(a−brm,t+1), b>1. When the equity premium is sufficiently low, b<1. a=0 and b=1 only holds when logE[Rm,t+1]−rf=Var(rm,t+1).We provide the proof in Appendix I.A.5. When Prediction 1 is true, then the distortions of the PME will be relevant for assets with a β different from one.If β is greater than one, then the PME understates the market equity premium and overestimates the abnormal return of the asset. The GPME corrects for this effect. Thus, when the PME overstates abnormal performance (PME>GPME) in a one-factor model, we can conclude that β is greater than one and the asset is pro-cyclical. More formally from Eq. (7), we derive the following prediction.Prediction 2 P2β. When the market equity premium is sufficiently high, PME overstates abnormal performance for high-β (β > 1) assets and understates abnormal performance for low-β (β < 1) assets in a one-factor model. If PME=GPME, then β=1.We use our predictions to compare the β of impact to the β of a set of benchmark funds. From Eq. (7), within the same time period, the gap between PME and GPME depends on the magnitude of β. Defining the PME wedge as PME−GPME, we make the following prediction.Prediction 3 P3Relative β. When the market equity premium is sufficiently high, and all else equal, the relative magnitude of the PME wedge reflects the relative magnitude of the asset’s β. If (PME−GPME)Benchmark>(PME−GPME)Impact, then βBenchmark>βImpact.We also use artificially levered cash flows to provide additional information on the magnitude of β. This exercise was first developed in Korteweg and Nagel (2016) as a robustness test for confirming the sign of β. We simulate increasing β by increasing a leverage factor k applied to fund cash flows and then estimating the PME and GPME of the levered cash flows using the original SDFs.The levered PME wedge is a direct amplification of the unlevered wedge plus an additional pricing error of PME on the risk-free replicating portfolios. We provide details for the properties of these cash flows and the corresponding wedge in Online Appendix I.A.3. Assuming jointly log-normal returns, the levered PME wedge reflects a sample estimate of the following estimator, which depends on β and the leverage factor k: (9)(βunlevered−1)(logE[Rm,t+1]−rf−σm2)+kβunlevered(logE[Rm,t+1]−rf−σm2)Therefore, the artificial leverage approach helps us generate the following predictions.Prediction 4 P4Levered β. When the market equity premium is sufficiently high, the PME wedge increases with k if βunlevered>0. The wedge decreases with k if βunlevered<0. Also, the relative slope of PME wedge with k reflects the relative magnitude of the asset’s β. If SlopeBenchmark>SlopeImpact, then βBenchmark>βImpact.Combining P2 and P4, we can back out ranges for β by looking at the plot of the PME wedge against the leverage factor k. If the wedge is decreasing, β<0; if the wedge is increasing but less than 0 at k=0, then 0<β<1; and if the wedge is increasing and greater than 0 at k=0, then β>1. Moreover, if the slope with respect to leverage is steeper for one asset than for another, the β for the underlying asset is higher. We use these predictions to determine the pro-cyclicality of impact investing in Section 4.3.4.2. Predictions for covariance with other factorsSo far we have limited ourselves to a CAPM model where the return on the market portfolio represents the return on a representative investor’s wealth portfolio. We may care about covariance with other factors, for example a public sustainability factor or an emerging market factor. We can use the same intuition for different public market indices that may reasonably capture alternative assets that constitute a representative investor’s wealth portfolio.We start by generalizing Eq. (7), which is reflected in the PME wedge under log-normal returns, to any public-market factor X. (10)(β−1)(logE[RX,t+1]−rf−σX2)We can also generalize Eq. (9), which is defined by the PME wedge at leverage factor k under lognormal returns: (11)(β−1)(logE[RX,t+1]−rf−σX2)+kβ(logE[RX,t+1]−rf−σX2)Our predictions now depend on the magnitude of the equity premium for X. The equity premium of public-market factor X is sufficiently high if (12)(logE[RX,t+1]−rf−σX2)>0Prediction 5 P5β for factor X. If the equity premium is sufficiently high for a public market factor X, then a positive PME wedge implies a βX>1 for that factor.Similarly, we can apply the logic of P4 and use artificial leveraging to back out the covariance structure of impact investing returns relative to public market factor X.Prediction 6 P6Levered β for factor X. If the equity premium is sufficiently high for a public market factor X, then a positive relationship between the PME wedge and the leverage factor k indicates a βX>0 for that factor.Finally, we can examine the risk profile of impact investing cash flows more generally using multifactor models, which might better reflect the tastes of investors. In these exercises, we examine what publicly traded factors span impact investing returns. To undertake this analysis, we test whether impact and benchmark cash flows have abnormal performance when discounted with SDFs that account for different risk factors.Prediction 7 P7Multifactor GPME. If impact investing returns are spanned by public market factors, then the GPME with respect to these factors is zero when cash flows are discounted with a multifactor SDF.A significant non-zero GPME with a multifactor SDF indicates that impact investing cannot be replicated with these public market factors.We use these predictions to determine the exposure of private market impact investing to alternative factors in Section 5.
article.4. Impact investing funds and market ris: Our first step for assessing the financial merits of impact investing is to measure impact investing strategies’ exposure to public market risk and their market risk-adjusted return. This analysis considers the case where the return on a representative investor’s wealth portfolio is approximated by the return on the market. We first examine private fund performance through the PME and GPME, then build on these results to examine the market risk exposure, or β, of each strategy. Lastly, we build long-short portfolios to sharpen the comparison across impact investing and benchmark funds.4.1. PME and GPME estimation with market factorWe use two SDFs to price impact and PE cash flows: the GPME is abnormal performance when the SDF Mt+1∗=exp(a−brm,t+1) is used to price cash flows, and the PME restricts this SDF to a special case where a=0 and b=1.We begin by estimating the parameters for Mt+1∗. We follow Korteweg and Nagel (2016) and create public market replicating portfolios of the risk-free rate and the gross market return, for each impact, matched, and VC fund in our sample. The purpose of these public market replicating portfolios is to create a cash flow series for an investor that invests in and receives distributions from public assets at roughly the same time intervals as the fund cash flow series we are interested in pricing. We then use GMM to find the parameters of the SDF such that the expected sum of discounted cash flows across all replicating portfolios is equal to zero. In order to create a consistent measure of performance, we price public market portfolios that replicate all of our sample fund cash flows (impact, matched, and VC funds together), and use the one set of parameters throughout our analysis. More details on the estimation method and the relevant assumptions can be found in Appendix A.The results of our estimation of SDF parameters are in Table 3. In the first column, the PME implicitly assumes that the coefficient associated with the risk-free rate (a) is 0 and the slope coefficient on the market return (b) is 1. Our estimates in the second column demonstrate that the ex-post SDF is very different from the PME assumptions. Using the procedure described above to estimate the realized SDF, we find the slope coefficient on the log market return (b) is 3.913 and the coefficient associated with the risk-free rate (a) is 0.199. These estimates are consistent with relatively “hot” equity markets in Prediction 1.14We now examine how the SDFs from Table 3 price the impact and matched fund cash flows. From Eq. (5), fund-level GPME is given by: GPMEi=∑j=1JMt+h(j)∗,h(j)Ci,t+h(j)for fund i, cash flow time j, cash flow horizon h(j), and cash flows C. Fund-level PME is given as: PMEi=∑j=1JMt+h(j)h(j),PMECi,t+h(j)In Table 4, we report the average performance of impact and matched funds under the PME and the market risk GPME SDF, using the SDF parameters from Table 3. We report standard errors in parentheses and p-values of the J-test of whether (G)PME=0 in brackets. For our J-test, we take a conservative approach and adjust the spectral density matrix for correlation between pricing errors and for estimation errors in subsamples with unequal lengths (to the best of our knowledge, this is new to our paper, and we provide details in Appendix A).Panel (a) presents our results for impact funds. We find a negative impact PME of −$0.21 per $1 of capital committed. The p-value is small, but this in part reflects the fact that the SDF parameters for the PME are assumed (a=0 and b=1) rather than estimated. We also find that the market risk GPME of impact funds is negative and statistically different from zero. After accounting for market risk and the equity premium environment, impact funds underperform the market index by $0.47 per $1. Using the direct alpha approach of Gredil et al. (2023) with our quarterly cash flows and estimated SDF, this GPME corresponds to an average quarterly risk-adjusted excess return of −8.8%. The overestimation of the PME relative to the GPME suggests that impact has a market β greater than one, in line with Prediction 2. The underperformance of impact investing relative to public markets is consistent with the general underperformance of constrained strategies, and the positive PME wedge with a pro-cyclical profile.We report the results for the matched benchmark funds in panel (b). Matched funds’ PME and GPME estimates are also negative, but less so than for impact funds: the PME of matched funds is −$0.01 per $1 capital committed and GPME is −$0.33, and we reject zero pricing errors for GPME with 10% significance. As in the impact fund sample, the PME overestimates the performance of matched funds relative to GPME. The PME wedge is larger for matched funds than for impact funds, suggesting that matched funds have larger market risk exposure. A public market equity investor would seem to do slightly better investing in the matched group of funds than in impact, but not significantly so. In line with this, we estimate an average direct alpha for matched funds of −4.4% per quarter. We evaluate the difference between impact and matched funds more directly through long-short portfolios in Section 4.3.In panel (c), we provide the same analysis for US VC funds. Impact and matched funds’ underperformance relative to the market appears to reflect the broader underperformance of VC over the same time period. The VC PME is also negative but less so than the impact PME, showing an abnormal loss of −$0.07 per $1 of capital committed. However, the VC GPME is close to the impact GPME, at a risk-adjusted loss of −$0.43 per $1 of capital committed. We can reject zero pricing errors for GPME. This GPME corresponds to a direct alpha estimate of −6.4% per quarter for VC funds.15 The results are consistent with previous literature in indicating a β of VC larger than one (Prediction 2) (Boyer et al., 2019, Cochrane, 2005). Our results suggest that an investor’s portfolio would perform about equally poorly by adding either impact or VC, if she cares about market risk exposure. We return to this claim in Section 4.3.The results in Table 4 represent averages for each strategy over a wide range of funds. Yet investors may be driven by the performance of top quartile funds (Harris et al., 2014, Korteweg and Sorensen, 2017). To understand the dispersion underlying our results, we compute fund-level α following Korteweg and Nagel (2022) for each of the three asset classes. As discussed in Appendix B, this approach requires different SDFs for each asset class, so comparisons should be taken with caution. We report the results in Table B.1 and Fig. B.1. Impact funds have a greater standard deviation than matched funds, and in particular a slightly longer right tail relative to matched funds. VC funds, which are much more numerous, have the largest standard deviation and right tail. In terms of market risk exposure, the α approach implies a β of 0.6 for impact funds and 1.8 for matched funds. The β for VC funds is even higher, at 2.5. Backing out market risk exposure using artificial leverageThe relatively small PME wedge of impact when compared to the other private market strategies suggests that impact funds have a low β with public markets. In this section, we use artificial leverage to bound the β of impact relative to non-impact options. We use the SDF estimates in Table 3 to price levered cash flows given in equation A.4, comparing PME and GPME estimates at each leverage point.We plot the PME wedge for impact, matched funds, and VC funds in Fig. 5. We create 95% confidence intervals by bootstrapping the wedge estimates 1000 times. At k=0, there is no additional leverage and the wedge is the difference between the PME and GPME estimates as in Section 4.1. As we increase k to a leverage factor of one and two, we artificially increase the β of the cash flows. Thus, for any asset with a positive β, we expect an increasing wedge with the addition of more leverage (Prediction 4). A β of zero would yield a constant wedge (horizontal line), as additional leverage does not affect the wedge magnitude. We can also apply negative leverage to the cash flows, replicating a decrease in β. As k approaches negative one, the wedge should become negative as levered β falls below one and the PME begins to understate performance.The dashed line for matched funds is consistent with Prediction 4 for a high-β asset: the PME wedge increases as the leverage factor increases. The solid line for impact funds also increases, but is flatter across leverage factors k than the matched fund line. We can reject a zero wedge at k=0 with 95% confidence, suggesting that β must be at least one, but the relatively flat slope of the wedge line indicates that β is not much higher than one. The higher sensitivity of the matched benchmark to leverage factors shows that impact’s lower β is not entirely explained by size, asset class, or vintage.To provide further context to this figure, we also plot a dotted line for the PME wedge of VC funds. The rapidly increasing line for VC is in line with literature estimates that place VC β substantially above one.16 This provides some reassurance that our approach yields reasonable estimates. The figure also shows that both matched and impact funds have significantly lower market risk exposure than VC funds. Although impact’s lower β is not entirely explained by size, asset class, or vintage, it seems that it is partly explained by these characteristics, underlining the importance of the matched fund comparison.We do not find impact is absolutely countercyclical: impact β is positive, and appears to be greater than one. Nonetheless, our results indicate impact fund returns are relatively less cyclical than those of comparable private market strategies. This finding is important as more investors consider adding impact to their portfolios, not only among VC investors but also among institutional investors like pension funds.17 The difference in β across the three strategies is economically meaningful. Looking at the PME in Table 4, matched funds appear to have almost zero pricing errors while impact funds have a significant negative return. Yet when we adjust for β using GPME, both strategies have significant negative returns, even if impact funds still yield a relatively lower return. The economic importance of adjusting for β is even more salient when we contrast impact with VC funds. VC funds clearly outperform impact funds on total returns (PME), but not so clearly on risk-adjusted returns (GPME). In the next section, we test the difference in risk exposure and risk-adjusted returns by constructing long-short portfolios.4.3. Comparing risk and return through long-short portfoliosWe directly test the performance and risk of impact relative to benchmark funds by creating portfolios of cash flows that go long $1 in benchmark funds and short $1 in impact funds. This is not a replicable strategy, but is meant to test relative differences in performance and risk between benchmark and impact funds. In this section, we look at value-weighted long-short portfolios to give us a better picture of the performance of the asset class as a whole. In Appendix I.B.2.2, we also provide results for equal-weighted long-short portfolios as statistical tests of the PME and GPME differences in Table 4. In addition to reporting returns, we artificially lever up the long-short portfolios to determine whether the strategies have a net positive or negative market β.4.3.1. Value-weighted long-short portfoliosWe price value-weighted long-short portfolios in order to better measure relative performance differences between a marginal dollar invested in non-impact and impact strategies. Conceptually, we invest $1 in the basket of benchmark funds available in our time period, and sell $1 in the basket of impact funds available during the same time.We create fund size-weighted portfolios of fund cash flows for each vintage year in the sample. These portfolios are constructed as a short portfolio of impact funds’ size-weighted cash flows subtracted from a long portfolio of benchmark funds’ size-weighted cash flows. The result is 14 weighted long-short portfolios of cash flows, one for each vintage year in the impact sample. We normalize each of the 14 portfolios such that the cash flows reflect that vintage’s size relative to other vintages. This is especially important for VC funds which have a different distribution of vintages relative to the impact fund sample.Table 5 presents the PME and GPME estimates of our value-weighted portfolios, using SDF estimates from Table 3. Panel (a) presents results using matched funds on the long side. The first column contains the PME estimates that assume the strategy has β=0, effectively discounting the long-short cash flows by the risk-free rate. The second column contains the GPME estimates. The PME estimate for a portfolio going long in matched funds and short impact funds is positive, $0.06 per $1 of committed capital, though we cannot reject zero pricing errors. When we adjust for market risk exposure with the GPME, the returns of going long matched funds and shorting impact are negative, −$0.02 per $1 of committed capital, but again we cannot reject zero pricing errors.18In Panel (b), we report the benchmark using VC funds on the long side. The PME results are positive, like those using matched funds, but the GPME results show VC funds actually perform worse than impact funds as a strategy during this period once we adjust for market risk exposure.19 Combining these results with the matched-impact comparison in panel (a) indicates that characteristics such as asset class and vintage play a role in impact’s performance over the time period.For an investor who cares about market risk exposure, impact’s performance as an asset class in our sample period is close to that of matched funds, and possibly better than that of US-focused VC funds. This finding is surprising, given that impact funds have an additional constraint relative to non-impact funds: their investments need to generate a social return in addition to a financial return. If investors can achieve similar returns by investing in impact opportunities, why not do that?One possibility is that private market investors care about absolute and not risk-adjusted returns. We normally think of a dollar being more valuable in downturns than expansions for most investors, but this may not reflect the preferences of investors in private markets, either because their wealth portfolio is different than the market, or because preferences are influenced by other factors than the marginal propensity to consume. We return to these questions in Section 5.Another possibility is that, given information frictions in private markets, investors are not fully informed about the opportunity set in impact investing. Cole et al. (2021) propose that impact funds’ access to certain markets, and imperfect integration of these with traditional markets, provide the possibility for impact funds to outperform. More broadly, market frictions like information barriers, investor biases, and imperfect competition (in capital or product markets) could be possible factors in impact’s relatively strong performance compared to benchmarks. Deal flow funnels for investors in matched and VC funds may be as restrictive as those for impact funds. For example, Gompers et al. (2020) find in their survey of VCs that healthcare VCs tend to consider fewer deals per investment than other VCs, reflecting both a smaller universe of healthcare entrepreneurs with domain expertise and larger fixed costs for evaluating healthcare deals. A VC may be a health fund which is constrained to only healthcare deals, while an impact fund may invest in any broadly defined environmental and socially responsible projects.Download: Download high-res image (159KB)Download: Download full-size imageFig. 6. Long short portfolio wedgesTo supplement this analysis, we also apply artificial leverage to the value-weighted long-short cash flow portfolios. Applying artificial leverage to the net cash flows is another way to test both whether the β of the benchmarks and impact are different and whether the β of the benchmarks are greater than the β of impact. If a benchmark and impact β are the same, then the β of that long-short portfolio should be zero, and the PME wedge should stay constant as we increase k. We formalize this intuition in Appendix I.A.6.What we observe in Fig. 6 is a wedge that is increasing in artificial leverage k for both long-short portfolios constructed in the previous section. The positive slopes of both the matched-impact LS portfolio (solid line) and the VC-impact LS portfolio (dashed line) indicate that the β of the net cash flows are positive: as k increases, the GPME becomes more negative and the wedge increases. This is further evidence that the β of both benchmarks is greater than the β of impact, although we note that our confidence intervals are too large to statistically reject a zero wedge at any value of the leverage factor. We see these results as an additional indication that impact investing provides lower market exposure than other private market strategies, including compared to matched funds.
article.5. Alternative models of risk and return: In this section, we discuss alternative models of risk and return in impact investing. First, we incorporate investor taste for public sustainable assets into the analysis of the risk and return of impact investing. Although sustainable investing is often discussed without distinguishing between public and private markets, investment opportunities in both markets are very different. We explore the extent to which adding a public sustainability factor can help explain the returns to impact investing. Next, we consider whether private market impact investing comoves with alternative benchmarks corresponding to different investor wealth portfolios, such as public sustainability and emerging markets.5.1. Taste for sustainabilityOur results so far document negative GPMEs for impact, matched, and VC funds, which equivalently means that all fund returns have negative CAPM αs. However, demand for sustainable assets in public markets might imply a different model of the risk–return trade-off for assets than the CAPM. Recent literature finds that when some investors have a preference for ESG, asset prices can be explained by a two-factor model that includes the market and an ESG factor, which captures unexpected changes in ESG concerns (Pastor et al., 2021). We therefore consider whether adding a public sustainability factor helps to explain the returns of private market impact and benchmark funds in our sample.We extend the one-factor GPME model to incorporate multiple factors (Prediction 7). The SDF in this case is Mt+1GPME=exp(a−b′log(Ft+1))where f is the number of public market factors, b is an f×1 vector of factor loadings, and log(Ft+1) is f×1 vector of public market factor returns at time t+1. Our analysis uses the simplest multi-factor GPME with f=2 to test whether impact, matched, and VC fund returns can be spanned by additional risk factors. We proxy for the public sustainability factor by using the Dow-Jones Sustainability World Index (DJSI World).Our main results are in Table 6. As in Table 4, Panel (a) provides estimates for impact funds, Panel (b) for matched funds, and Panel (c) for VC funds. In the first column, we repeat the GPME estimates from the one-factor market risk model as a reference. The second column corresponds to the GPME estimates for the two-factor SDF with the market and public sustainability index. In both columns, we report standard errors in parentheses and p-values for the J-test of whether the GPME=0 in brackets. To test whether the multifactor models span returns, we focus on whether the J-test implies that we cannot reject zero pricing errors.Our first observation is that the GPME estimate for impact funds moves closer to zero when we add the public sustainability index to the market factor. In the single market factor model, we reject zero pricing errors, finding an abnormal loss of −$0.47 relative to the market. In the two-factor model with public sustainability, the estimate goes up to −$0.38. We can still reject zero pricing errors but only at a 10% significance level.Our second observation is that in contrast to the results for impact funds, adding the public sustainability index does not seem to improve the model for matched funds. Matched funds lose −$0.33 per $1 of capital committed relative to the market; this loss moves to −$0.34 per $1 committed when we risk-adjust using the market and sustainability multifactor model. For VC funds, the GPME estimate moves to −$0.40 lost per $1 of capital committed compared to −$0.43 in the single-factor CAPM model.We conclude that the additional explanatory power of the public sustainability factor for impact funds is not the mechanical result of adding a factor. Instead, impact funds appear to have a distinct risk exposure from benchmark funds, that is captured by adding the public sustainability index to the market factor. We explore this in further detail in the next section.5.2. Alternative wealth portfoliosAlternative models of impact risk and return may also take the form of one-factor models with different single factors, corresponding to different investor wealth portfolios. For example, the portfolios of sustainability-motivated individuals may be constructed so that the return on their wealth portfolio is best approximated by the return on a public sustainability index, rather than the return on the market. In that case, it is important to consider the performance of impact investing (and benchmark strategies) relative to the factor that best represents investors’ wealth portfolios (Sorensen and Jagannathan, 2015). Alternative one-factor models also allow us to paint a richer picture of the risk properties of impact investing by providing alternative βs, estimates of comovement with other factors.We measure the exposure of impact funds to two separate factors that may be relevant financial risks for impact investor limited partners, public sustainability and emerging markets. We show that impact investing has distinct risk characteristics along these dimensions as well, and that alternative wealth portfolios can imply different assessments of whether impact is concessionary.5.2.1. Public sustainabilityWe first consider a scenario where the alternative wealth portfolio for investors is not the market, but the publicly traded sustainability index. We imagine this to be an appropriate portfolio for some sustainability-motivated high-income individuals. This alternative model with the public sustainability index (DJSI World) as the single factor overlaps to some extent with the CAPM model because we do not partial out the market. The difference is that this allows for a wealth portfolio that emphasizes companies with strong economic, environmental and social practices. While the two-factor model accounts for the correlation between the market and public sustainability index, the one-factor model allows us to evaluate the risk exposure to the public sustainability factor directly, as well as decompose performance in a different way.Table 7 reports the one-factor SDF estimate using the sustainability index as the public market return of interest. The estimates show that the ex-post SDF using public sustainability as the public market alternative (column 2) is different from the SDF with log-utility assumptions (column 1). The fact that b is greater than one indicates that the return premium is “sufficiently high” (Prediction 1). The wedge between PME and GPME informs us about comovements with the model’s factor, and a positive wedge corresponds with positive comovement. Table 8 provides the PME and GPME estimates from this alternative one-factor model. Both PME and GPME estimates differ widely among impact, matched, and VC funds. With risk adjustment relative to the public sustainability index, matched funds gain $0.23 per $1 capital committed, while VC funds lose $0.08 and impact funds lose $0.51 per $1 capital committed (we can only reject zero pricing errors for the latter). For an investor whose wealth portfolio is captured by the public sustainability index, impact funds are not financially profitable portfolio additions.Table 6. article.6. Conclusion: In this paper, we provide a characterization of the risk profile and risk-adjusted performance of impact investing. To do this we develop a new statistic to derive risk properties of private market asset classes, the “PME wedge”, building on insights from Korteweg and Nagel (2016). While we apply it to impact investing in this paper, our approach can easily be extended to examining other private market strategies in the future.Our finding that the β of impact is lower than the β of benchmark private market funds is consistent with a less cyclical interpretation of impact strategies. As more investors consider incorporating impact funds into their portfolios, it will matter how these funds contribute to investors’ overall investment strategies. Our results show that adding impact investing to a portfolio adds less market risk exposure than adding comparable private market strategies (funds matched on asset class, size, and vintage).For investors who care about market risk exposure, our results also have implications for performance. Although matched and VC funds have higher absolute performance than impact funds, once we account for market risk exposure, we find risk-adjusted performance across the strategies is closer. Value-weighted portfolios long in matched funds and short impact have returns that cannot be statistically distinguished from zero after removing the component attributable to market risk exposure.Lastly, we provide evidence that impact investing performance depends on investor tastes and their wealth portfolios. Adding a public sustainability index to the market in a two-factor model improves our ability to explain impact fund returns, but does not change results for matched or VC funds. At the same time, a one-factor model with the public sustainability factor alone shows that impact funds have low covariance with public sustainability. Additionally, impact funds have positive exposure to emerging markets, while benchmark funds have a negative covariance with an emerging markets index. These findings help us paint a richer picture of impact investing as a strategy with its own financial risk profile.Our results can also be understood as a decomposition of total returns. Total returns using IRRs or multiples are highest for matched funds, followed by VC funds, and with impact funds last. We show that a large part of that difference can be attributed to different market risk exposure: after accounting for market risk, the residual performance is much more similar across the three sets of funds. Adding a public sustainability factor to the market factor further helps to explain the returns of impact investing funds, though it does not improve the pricing model for benchmark funds. On the other hand, decomposing returns around a sustainability factor alone or around an emerging markets factor does not reduce return differences between impact and benchmark funds. Our findings highlight the importance of reference points in the debate over impact investing performance. We hope in future work to further explore the distinctiveness of the impact profile achievable in private markets, and how it connects to the financial risk and return of impact investing funds.
